title: What Different Heads Learn
concept: Visualization shows head 1 focuses on subject-verb agreements, head 2 on pronoun reference, and head 3 on punctuation relations.

exercises.prompt: What are "attention heads" in a Transformer model?

options.text: Independent focus points that look for different patterns
The physical cameras on an AI robot
Different computer screens showing the same text

exercises.explain: Attention heads are independent mechanisms inside a Transformer that allow it to focus on different parts of a sentence simultaneously.

cards.title: Split the Work!
cards.body: If you and your friends read the exact same book, you might focus on different things—one tracks the plot, another notices the grammar, and someone else looks at the punctuation. Transformer AI does the exact same thing using different 'heads'!

cards.title: What is Head Specialization?
cards.body: Inside a Transformer, "attention heads" are independent focus points. Instead of the whole model looking at everything at once, different heads specialize in tracking specific relationships, like matching subjects with verbs, linking pronouns to nouns, or checking punctuation.

cards.title: The Detective Trio
cards.body: Imagine a group of three detectives studying the exact same crime scene photo. Detective 1 only looks at footprints (subject-verb matches). Detective 2 only tracks fingerprints (pronouns and who they point to). Detective 3 only checks the security cameras (punctuation marks). Together, their specialized eyes solve the case!

cards.title: Seeing it in Action
cards.body:
In the sentence: "The dog, which was very tired, chased its tail."

• Head 1 links "dog" with "chased" (Subject-Verb).
• Head 2 links "its" back to "dog" (Pronoun Reference).
• Head 3 connects the comma "," to the rest of the clause (Punctuation relation).

cards.title: Do They Do the Same Job?
cards.body: Many people think all attention heads do the exact same calculation to get a safer average. In reality, they automatically self-organize to divide and conquer, focusing on completely different grammatical rules!

cards.title: Visualizing the Focus
cards.body: Visualizing attention maps reveals how different heads light up for different linguistic features.

left.text: Lights up connections between words like 'chickens' and 'cluck'.

right.text: Lights up connections between words like 'she' and 'Sarah'.

exercises.prompt: If Head 2 is specialized in pronoun references, what is its main job when reading a sentence?

options.text: Linking pronouns like "she" or "it" back to the correct noun
Counting how many letters are in a word
Capitalizing the first letter of the sentence

exercises.explain: Head 2 is specialized in pronoun references, which means it helps the model figure out who or what a word like "he", "she", or "it" is referring to.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Visualization shows that Head 1, Head 2, and Head 3 focus on different grammatical relations in a sentence.
exercises.explain: This is true. Visualization tools show that different heads naturally focus on different linguistic structures, such as subject-verb relationships, pronouns, or punctuation.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how attention heads divide their work.
exercises.sentence: Instead of doing the same task, different attention heads have _____ roles.
exercises.explain: Different attention heads develop specialized roles, focusing on distinct relationships like subject-verb agreement or pronoun tracking.

exercises.prompt: Which of these simulated attention heads behaves like a real Transformer attention head?

options.text: Head A: "I count the total number of vowels in every word to check if they match."
Head B: "I connect the subject noun to its matching action verb across the sentence."
Head C: "I simply repeat the first word of the sentence over and over again."

exercises.explain: Head B is the AI assistant because it demonstrates grammatical specialization (tracking subject-verb pairs), which is how real transformer attention heads behave. Head A is just performing a simple character count.

exercises.prompt: If you opened up a transformer model with three attention heads, what would you most likely observe them doing while reading a sentence?

options.text: They all look at the exact same words in the same way to double-check each other's math.
One head reads the text forward, another reads it backward, and the third translates it.
Each head focuses on a different type of connection, like finding subject-verb pairings or pronoun links.
They convert words into random numbers and ignore grammar entirely.

exercises.explain: Attention heads work in parallel, with each head naturally specializing in a specific grammatical or structural relation rather than doing the exact same task.
